steps.title: Identify the timeline signal
steps.content: The phrase “Before that” is the key. “That” refers to when he watched North by Northwest at last year's festival and wrote the scathing review. “Before that” tells us we're describing what was true at an earlier point in the past.

steps.title: Apply the tense rule for prior past events
steps.content:
When one past event happened before another past event, use past perfect: had + past participle. Here, A Beautiful Mind was least enjoyed before he watched North by Northwest (the festival — the later past event).
  • Had been (B) — past perfect: establishes the prior state correctly. ✓
  • Is (A) — simple present: describes the current moment, not any past state.
  • Was being (C) — past progressive: describes an action unfolding over time in the past (e.g., "the film was being screened"), not a completed prior state.
  • Has been (D) — present perfect: describes something continuing from the past into the present; but the sentence is set entirely in the past, so present perfect is incorrect.
That matches choice B — B is correct.
